text: Robotic repair of a recurrent lumbar hernia. The objective of this presentation is to provide the learner with the operative approach and technique for robotic repair of a recurrent lumbar hernia.

Lumbar hernias can form in either the inferior lumbar triangle, which lies superficially, or the superior lumbar triangle, which is deep. Of the two, the superior triangle is more commonly the site of herniation. The superior lumbar triangle is formed medially by the sacrospinalis, laterally by the posterior border of the internal abdominal oblique, and superiorly by the twelfth rib. The floor of the superior lumbar triangle is the transversalis fascia, and its roof is the external abdominal oblique.

The margins of the inferior lumbar triangle are comprised of the iliac crest, latissimus dorsi, and external abdominal oblique. The floor of the inferior lumbar triangle is the internal abdominal oblique.

Our patient is a fifty-five year old woman with a BMI of twenty-one point three. who presented with a recurrent left posterior bulge. She reported that she had a posterior lumbar hernia repair in in that did not involve mesh. The hernia recurred shortly thereafter, but she did not seek treatment for several years. However, it started to enlarge and become more symptomatic, so she has now requested surgical repair. Preoperative abdominal CT shows a fat-containing, two point seven by two point five centimeter left lumbar hernia.

The patient is positioned in the steep reverse Trendelenburg in the right lateral decubitus position with arms extended. A 12mm trocar is inserted in the left upper quadrant using the Hassan technique, lateral to the semilunar line and 2-3 cm below the costal margin. The abdomen is insufflated, and an 8mm robotic camera with a 30-degree angle is inserted. Three additional 8mm trocars are placed along the left hemiabdomen to enhance visualization and facilitate adhesiolysis.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum three centimeters lateral to the colonic reflection and dissects through the retroperitoneal fat to identify the transversus abdominis muscle. The dissection proceeds in the avascular plane, extending cranially onto the diaphragm and down into the pelvis past the ASIS. The dissection continues medially onto the quadratus lumborum and psoas muscle, with clear identification and preservation of the subcostal nerve. The retroperitoneal fat is visibly herniating out of the posterior lumbar defect, with the edges of the hernia defect clearly defined.
The surgeon cuts and removes a stitch from the previous repair, revealing a hernia defect measuring three by two centimeters.
The surgeon attempts to close the posterior lumbar defect using a 1-0 V-lock suture. The suture visibly pulls through the tissue, indicating its friable nature.

A seven and a half by fifteen centimeter barred soft mesh is placed into the abdominal cavity, centered over the hernia defect with overlap in each direction. The mesh is secured using a two-oh vikril stitch and is visualized to ensure it lies completely flat in the posterior space.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum using a running two-oh V-lock suture, ensuring the tissue edges are approximated smoothly.
The final visualization of the repaired area shows the surgical site with no visible signs of complications or recurrent hernia.
